text: The robotic keyhole thymectomy begins with three ports on the left side: two 8mm and one 12mm. An additional 8mm port may be used on the right side to confirm the phrenic nerve position. The surgeon starts dissection medial to the left phrenic nerve, moving superiorly to expose the left innominate vein. The left thymic horn is pulled down to reach the inferior pole of the thyroid, where the vein is clipped off.

The surgeon mobilizes the left thymic lobe off the precordium, following the innominate vein towards the right thymic horn. The right thymic horn is drawn into the chest, clipped, and diathermy is applied. The procedure emphasizes the removal of all thymic tissue between both phrenic nerves and from the thyroid to the diaphragm to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon begins dissection at the start of the superior vena cava (SVC), focusing on the tributaries of the thymic veins and arteries as they connect to the internal mammary vein and artery. The right pleura is opened to improve exposure and retraction. The thymus is mostly mobilized, and the surgeon releases it from the inferior portion just above the diaphragm, targeting the most inferior part of the right thymic lobe.

The surgeon moves the entire thymus en bloc into the left pleura and introduces a retrieval bag through the twelve millimeter port. The thymus is visible at the edge of the port, and the innominate vein and superior vena cava (SVC) are completely denuded of thymic tissue between the phrenic nerves.
